title: Index and key page design
subtitle: Designing a functional index page and symbol key for structured journals
excerpt: An index page at the front of a journal and a key or legend for symbols and colour coding turn a filled notebook into a searchable reference. Both are simple to set up on blank or dot-grid pages. The design decisions that make them work are about layout, not decoration.
sourceNotes: Index and key page designs are documented in contemporary bullet journal and structured journal craft literature from the 2010s onward. No pre-1928 primary source applies. Synthesised from contemporary craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A page at the front of a journal that lists the contents with corresponding page numbers, allowing the reader to locate specific entries.
A page that defines symbols, colour codes, and abbreviations used consistently throughout a journal.

content.text: are the two navigational pages that turn a journal from a dated record into a usable reference system. Neither requires calligraphy or decoration. Both require forward planning in the layout.
Two blank pages reserved at the front of the journal, before any dated entries. Page-number the entire journal first: number all pages at the outer bottom corner in pencil before writing any content.
Draw a header: the word INDEX and the journal's date range or number.
Rule the page into two columns. Left column: 80% of the page width, for entry titles. Right column: 20% for page numbers.
Leave the index blank until entries are written. Add each significant entry as you complete it, not at the end of the journal.
Optional: alphabetise by subject rather than by page order. Rule alphabetical sections with letters A to Z down the left margin and allow two to three lines per letter.
Decide on a symbol set before starting the journal. Common choices: a dot for a task, a circle for an event, a dash for a note, a star for a priority, a question mark for something to look up.
Draw each symbol at a size that matches the scale of the journal's body text. Too small and the symbols blur into the background.
Define a colour code if you use coloured pens or pencils. Four to six colours is a manageable maximum; more becomes difficult to remember without consulting the key constantly.
Keep the key stable. A symbol that changes meaning mid-journal makes previous entries unreadable.

For a large journal (200 pages or more), use a two-page index spread and add a secondary alphabetical index at the back. For a junk journal or scrapbook, the index might list projects rather than individual entries: photograph the spread, label it, and index by project name.
